chapters.summary: Council staff opens the hearing of the Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management, reminds attendees not to approach the dais and to silence electronic devices, and invites the Chair to begin.
chapters.title: Hearing called to order and housekeeping

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's New York City hearing on Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, during today's hearing, no one may approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please silence any electronic devices that you may have.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, you may begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Justin Sanchez opens the hearing, apologizes for the delayed start, thanks attendees for their patience, and acknowledges council members present including Lewis, Zhuang, Carr, Thomas-Henry, and Maloney.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez welcomes committee and apologizes for delay

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to the Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I am Justin Sanchez, Council Member and Chair of this committee.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today and truly, truly, truly apologize for the delay in this morning's proceedings.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: That is not the way that we like to operate on this committee, so really apologize and thank you for everyone for being here on time.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: It is truly appreciated.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And also working with us to change the committee to an earlier time because the entire plan was to get everyone out earlier if I hadn't been so late.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So thank you everyone and I'd also like to acknowledge the council members who are with us here today.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Council member Lewis, council member Zhuang, council member Carr.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Minority Leader Carr, and Councilmember Shanel-Thomas-Henry, and I know that Councilmember Virginia-Maloney was also here and will be returning to join us shortly.

chapters.summary: Sanchez summarizes the purpose of the hearing: six bills including Intro 75 (sidewalk cleanliness enforcement), Intro 353 (commercial waste zone working group), Intro 356 (community composters charging/selling), Intro 367 (commercial waste at city transfer stations), Intro 369 (organic waste composting facilities per borough), and Intro 911A (Staten Island CWZ awardees and implementation delay).
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez outlines the six bills on today's agenda

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So, the purpose of today's hearing is to hear six bills, five of which are sponsored by Councilmember Sandy Nurse.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Intro number 75 would amend the Department of Sanitation's enforcement routing program to allow for businesses to receive fines for sidewalk cleanliness violations during their hours of operations, or between the hours of 8 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And 7 p.m.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Intro number 353 would establish a commercial waste zone working group.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Intro number 356 would require DSNY to adopt rules allowing community composters to charge the public for organic waste collection at drop-off sites.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: community composters to sell compost generated from drop-off sites to the public.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Intro number 367 would require DSNY to accept and process commercial solid waste at all city-owned or operated marine and rail transfer stations and to publicly report the amount

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: and type of waste received at such stations on an annual basis.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And intro number 369 would require DSNY to establish one or more organic waste composting facilities in each borough.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: We will also be hearing intro 911A, sponsored by Councilmember David Carr, which would require DSNY to enter into agreements with six commercial waste haulers for the borough of Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And delay implementation of the Staten Island commercial waste zone until February 1st, 2028.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I will now turn it over to Councilmember Carr to provide a statement on his bill.

chapters.summary: Council Member David Carr presents Intro 911A, arguing Staten Island had a functioning commercial carting market that did not need the CWZ program. He expresses concern about reduced consumer choice, local businesses being boxed out, and companies inflating value for sale before fulfilling awards. He urges a pause on CWZ implementation and expanding to six carters for Staten Island.
chapters.title: Minority Leader Carr introduces Intro 911A on Staten Island CWZ

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I appreciate you having this hearing today, and I really want to commend you on your leadership of the committee throughout the course of this year.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I look forward to continuing to work with you in improving our quality of life across all of our boroughs.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I just want to welcome our friend here today.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I can promise you my cats, if they were here, would not be as well-behaved as he is.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Commissioner, always a pleasure to see you.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I really appreciate your partnership since you returned to the agency and working on a whole host of issues to improve quality of life for Staten Islanders.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I really want to thank everyone at the Sanitation Department, particularly in the borough operations in Staten Island and Brooklyn, for the phenomenal job they do each and every week.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Introduction 911A is introduced, I think, to resolve a problem that has arisen as a result of the implementation of the commercial waste zones.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: In my view, when I was a part of the work of my predecessor, Steve Mattio, he opposed this legislation because he felt that it was a...

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Solution to a problem in some parts of our city, but not one that we were facing in Staten Island and yet created negative externalities for our borough.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: We had a perfectly working market situation in Staten Island with respect to commercial carding and

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: there were not the negative consequences that you're seeing in other places where you had multiple carters serving blocks, different businesses had different contracts, and the negative results that attended to that, namely congestion,

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: truck traffic and noise at unfortunate hours, and just repeated visits by different trucks on the same block.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: That was never something we experienced on Staten Island, and so this is a solution that is great for other parts of the city and not necessarily one that was needed in Staten Island, but we are now limited as Staten Islanders in who we can choose to contract with.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Generally speaking against those kind of market interferences because I think consumers should have a choice that they should have a choice as to who their Carter is and in particular I'm concerned about the possibility

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: that long-time Staten Island businesses would then be cut out of a market in which they have been ably serving for a number of

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: years.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Additionally, you know, I'm concerned about one of the other consequences of this legislation, which is that before

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: a CWZ could even be implemented or before an award could even become operational, you have companies that are seeking to sell before they even begin the work of their award.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that creates the possibility

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: where an awardee inflates the value of their company in order to be sold and without ever having done any of the work.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that's something that we have to guard against.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: We have to protect the department.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: We have to protect the taxpayer.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And we have to protect the consumers in each of the zones.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And so that is why I'm putting forward Introduction 911A because I think we need a pause on the implementation of the CWZ.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think we also need to make sure that Staten Islanders have as many choices as they can.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think the unique circumstances that we have in our borough justify doing things differently there than we would do in the other commercial waste zones.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And so I urge my colleagues to be supportive of this legislation, and I look forward to hearing testimony from the department.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Sanchez recognizes Councilmember Vernikov on Zoom and Maloney in person, then turns to committee counsel to administer the oath to Commissioner Gregory Anderson, Assistant Commissioner Frank Marshall, and Director Jen McDonnell.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez recognizes additional members and administers oath to DSNY witnesses

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Minority Leader Carr.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I look forward to the dialogue today, and I would like to thank everyone on my team for their work on this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: We will start by, oh, I also want to recognize Councilmember Vernikoff, who has joined us via Zoom, and recognize that Councilmember Maloney is now with us in person.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: We will start by hearing testimony from DSNY, then we will turn it over to

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: hear testimony from members of the public, and I will now turn it over to our committee counsel to administer the oath.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning, Commissioner Anderson, Assistant Commissioner Marshall, and Director Jen McDonnell.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm, may you please raise your right hands?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth when testifying and responding to council member questions today?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you, you may begin.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Anderson celebrates the Council's passage of Intro 31 (commercial organics expansion) and Intro 92 (ASP stickers), announces Brooklyn Community District 2 containerization signups, the publication of the draft EIS for containerization, and the start of customer signup for Midtown South and Staten Island commercial waste zones.
chapters.title: Commissioner Anderson opens with agency updates on organics, containerization, and CWZ signup

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Sanchez and members of the Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I'm Gregory Anderson, Commissioner of the New York City Department of Sanitation, and I'm joined by Jen McDonald, Deputy Commissioner for Solid Waste Management, and Frank Marshall, Assistant Commissioner for the Bureau of Commercial Waste.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify in front of this committee today.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Before I get into my prepared testimony, I just want to provide a few updates.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: There's a lot of exciting things happening in sanitation.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: First, happy fiscal new year to everyone.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We were very excited to see yesterday that the City Council passed Intro 31, which would allow sanitation to expand the commercial organics program to all food-related businesses.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And we look forward to implementing that over the course of the next few months ahead of the December 1st deadline in the bill.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And second, we are very excited that the council passed Intro 92, sponsored by our dear friend, Councilmember Gail Brewer, to allow us to use stickers to

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: alert the public of violators of alternate side parking.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So we look forward to rolling that out in the coming months as well.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: As it is July 1st, we have exciting news around containerization.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: First, today is the first day of signups and registration for Brooklyn Community District 2.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So all businesses with 10 or more units in Brooklyn Community District 2 will be able to sign up to receive their Empire bins.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: They're mandatory for buildings 31 plus units and they are optional for buildings with 10 to 30 units.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: They can choose either the Empire bins or wheelie bins, whichever they prefer.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: They just have to let us know which they prefer.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So we will start doing outreach today.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We have teams out on the ground, and I'm heading over there shortly after this hearing to film a quick little video.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Secondly, on containerization, very excited to announce that we have published our draft environmental impact statement for the containerization program.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: This is something that the department has worked on tirelessly for the last year plus.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: It's the result of an incredible amount of analysis, public engagement, and we really want to thank our team who's worked on this, as well as our partner agencies at DOT, DEP,

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Mayor's Office of Environmental Coordination, and others who have been part of this process.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And we will have a hearing on the final scope of work in the DEIS coming up in July.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And lastly,

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: It is July 1st, as I mentioned, which means that starting today, customers in the Midtown South and Staten Island commercial waste zones can sign up with one of their three selected carters.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And that sign-up period continues through August 31st.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So we are also having outreach staff out there today.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: It'll be limited for the next couple days because of the heat wave, but starting next week we'll be out there in full force.

chapters.summary: Anderson expresses strong support for Intro 75, explaining it fixes a historic limitation restricting enforcement to two one-hour windows. He emphasizes the goal is compliance, not revenue, and describes outreach and education efforts with SBS, BIDs, and chambers of commerce.
chapters.title: DSNY testifies in support of Intro 75 on sidewalk cleanliness enforcement

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So turning to the bills we have in front of us today, first, Intro 75, sponsored by Councilmember Nurse, would allow sanitation to conduct cleanliness enforcement for commercial premises during all posted business hours, or from

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: 8 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: To 7 p.m.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: For businesses that don't have posted hours.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We strongly support this legislation, which we think will improve the cleanliness of our commercial corridors.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: This addresses a longstanding issue that currently restricts our enforcement of dirty sidewalks to just two one-hour periods per day.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And for commercial properties, this limitation can prevent us from performing effective enforcement for some of the worst actors, those individual businesses that can mar the appearance of an entire commercial corridor.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: This legislation would fix this historic wrong and allow for common sense and consistent enforcement during a business's posted operating hours.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Our goal, to be clear, is never enforcement for enforcement's sake, nor is it for the sake of revenue, for that matter, as well.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Our goal is and always will be to achieve compliance and cleanliness, particularly among repeat offenders.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And we thank the council for their action in the last few years to try to give us more tools in this area.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Our outreach and business engagement staff work closely with our partner agencies like SBS, our business improvement districts, chambers of commerce, and other stakeholders to ensure that education remains our first tool to achieve that compliance

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: and to give New York City businesses the information tools they need to comply with longstanding sanitation laws.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: All property owners have a responsibility to keep their sidewalks and their properties clean, and DSNY is grateful to Councilmember Nurse and Chair Sanchez for their leadership in advancing this important bill.

chapters.summary: Anderson welcomes the working group concept in Intro 353 and says DSNY plans to advance it regardless. On Intro 356, he says current rules do not prohibit charging for organic waste drop-off or selling compost, and with prior city-funded restrictions no longer in effect, the bill is not necessary at this time.
chapters.title: DSNY on Intro 353 (CWZ working group) and Intro 356 (community composter fees)

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Intro 353, sponsored by Councilmember Nurse, would require the creation of a commercial waste zones working group.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: There's a lot of words here in my testimony on this.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I'll just skip to the end, which is...

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We certainly welcome the opportunity to conduct additional engagement through a formalized working group and plan to advance the formalized working group whether or not this bill moves.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So we'll look forward to working with the council to get that done.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: For Intro 356, again sponsored by Councilmember Nurse, would require sanitation to adopt rules to permit

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: community partners to charge for the collection of organic waste at drop-off site and to sell compost to the public.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: DSNY rules currently do not prohibit this practice.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Previous sanitation

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: contracts with certain community partners did prohibit programs funded by the city from charging for these services, but because those programs are no longer baseline funded in the administration's budget, these restrictions no longer apply.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So I think

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: You know, the takeaway here is we look forward to working with the council to understand the intent of this legislation, but we don't think it's necessary at this time.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And given that the council has provided significant funding on the order of $5.25 million in the Schedule C posted yesterday, we do think that

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: publicly funded programs do need to balance access equity with the financial sustainability of the organizations that are funded by the council.

chapters.summary: Anderson states DSNY opposes Intro 367 as written but has committed conceptually to accepting commercial waste at marine and rail transfer stations in both the 2006 and 2026 solid waste management plans. He says the department plans to begin this process after full CWZ implementation by end of 2027.
chapters.title: DSNY opposes Intro 367 on commercial waste at city transfer stations

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Intro 367, sponsored by Councilmember Nurse, would require sanitation to accept commercial waste at our city's transfer stations.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And while we oppose the bill as it is written, we have said previously and committed conceptually to the acceptance of commercial waste at our transfer stations in both the 2006 final and 2026 draft solid waste management plans.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And the permits for these facilities do allow them to accept commercial waste today.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: As we said at the swamp hearing in the beginning of April, we plan to begin this process after we finish the complete citywide implementation of commercial waste zones by the end of 2027.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And we look forward to continuing these conversations as we approach that date next year.

chapters.summary: Anderson opposes Intro 369's requirement for composting facilities in each borough with 180,000 wet ton capacity, citing regulatory, operational, and fiscal realities that make implementation impossible. He notes openness to conversations about expanding composting capacity.
chapters.title: DSNY opposes Intro 369 on per-borough composting facilities

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Next, Intro 360.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: 9 would require sanitation to establish one or more organic waste composting facilities in each borough with the capacity to process at least 100,000 wet ton 180,000 wet tons of organic waste annually

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: and while we appreciate the council's interest in expanding processing capacity citywide we do oppose the bill's requirement for compost facilities in each borough with this capacity

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: given the simple regulatory operational and fiscal realities that make that would make implementation impossible

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So we are happy to have further conversations with council about our efforts to expand composting capacity, but we don't think this bill would be implementable.

chapters.summary: Anderson opposes Intro 911A, saying it would undermine the CWZ program's core benefits of decreased truck traffic, improved safety, and consumer protections. He notes the Staten Island zone implementation started that morning and opposes disrupting the system mid-implementation.
chapters.title: DSNY opposes Intro 911A on Staten Island CWZ expansion and delay

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And lastly, Intro 911A, sponsored by Councilmember Carr, would increase the number of carters in the Staten Island commercial waste zone from no more than three to a total of six and delay the implementation of such zone.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Sanitation opposes this bill as it would undermine the commercial waste zone program.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Its core benefits of decreased truck traffic, improved safety, and strengthened consumer protections.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And we cannot support the disruption to this system in the midst of implementation, particularly, as I mentioned previously, with the Staten Island Zone implementation starting this morning.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Nor do we support depriving Staten Island residences and businesses from the clear benefits this program provides, which we've seen in the six zones that are already implemented.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So thank you for the opportunity to testify today, and I am now happy to answer your questions.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I will turn it over first to...

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Minority leader Carr to ask about intro 911.

chapters.summary: Carr presses Anderson on whether expanding to six carters would undermine CWZ benefits, arguing new awardees would still meet program standards. Anderson counters that CWZ brings safety, recycling compliance, and service quality benefits, and that making exceptions creates a slippery slope.
chapters.title: Carr questions Anderson on benefits of CWZ for Staten Island

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And Commissioner, you mentioned containerization earlier.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I just want to thank you again for extending both the funding and the grace period for individual compliance and righting a wrong of the previous administration and making sure folks have time to actually get the bins.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So thank you again for that.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I know you worked closely with the Chair and Councilmember Morano on getting that done.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: With respect to Intro 911A, I think one of the things that I've been concerned about is that there's no real attendant benefit for Staten Island because it's, again, as I said in my statement, you're not resolving a problem that we had.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: It was a problem that existed in other boroughs.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And more importantly, I dispute the notion that whatever benefits that do come with the program are being disrupted by...

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: This legislation because I'm not actually seeking to change the standards by which additional awardees would be selected.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So could you kind of describe what your concerns are from your perspective with what we're proposing here today?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, so I would dispute the notion that there's no benefit to Staten Island from commercial waste zones.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I think the benefits are

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: many.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And while the truck traffic concern may not have been as great on Staten Island as it was in other places, I think that there are very real concerns around safety for both workers and the public.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: There are very real concerns around the lack of compliance with commercial

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: commercial recycling requirements and commercial organics requirements.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And I think very, very serious concerns around the kind of services that haulers that had been operating on Staten Island and across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: In the prior system.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And so commercial waste zones brings a number of benefits, not just reducing the total truck traffic, but that is certainly a significant one.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And the program parameters writ large, I think, bring a number of benefits to Staten Islanders.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: But if there were new awardees,

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: they would have to comply with the requirements of the program.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: If they were an existing hauler already operating, they would now have to comply with the standards that need to be met for any commercial waste zone awardee.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So I'm struggling to understand how bringing additional folks into the system for consumers to choose would...

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Would denude a lot of the additional requirements that they would have to meet.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: They would have to meet the same standards as any other awardee.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So I don't think that there's really a diminishment in the benefits of the CWZ.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: If anything, you're just bringing more folks into compliance who may not have been before, and yet they continue to operate.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Consumers continue to have more choices.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I think that there's...

chapters.summary: Carr argues 21% of Staten Island businesses must find a new carter unnecessarily, and that the bill preserves consumer choice without diminishing CWZ standards. Anderson responds that 79% already use one of the three selected carters, the program was debated for years, and exceptions would invite 'what about me' demands from others.
chapters.title: Carr and Anderson debate consumer choice and Staten Island's unique circumstances

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I think there's no trade-off here between the goals of the authors of the CWZ program and what I'm trying to do here.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, I think the challenge is that

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We have been working to implement this for seven years now, and this bill is getting a hearing on the day that the Staten Island Zone is being implemented.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: A full two years plus after these contracts were awarded, a full six years after the RFP was released.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And I think, just to be frank, it is a slippery slope once you start to make exceptions for certain parts of the city or certain carters in a program like this.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I think our concern would be that others would stand up and say, well, what about me?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: What about me?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And the commercial waste zone system was designed very, very,

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: deliberately and carefully by the council and the sanitation department over the course of many, many years in order to build a system that works, that operate, that gives businesses choice.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: There are many places across the country, particularly in the South, in Florida, in Texas, in Los Angeles, where businesses only get one choice.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: For their hauler.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We specifically said that's not acceptable.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We want to give businesses choice, and today businesses on Staten Island can choose any of those three carters that they want to provide their waste collection services.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And 79% of businesses on Staten Island already use one of those three carters.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So it's really only 21% of businesses on Staten Island that have to pick a new carter.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We're confident that the three carters that are out there today are able to provide very high quality, very low cost services for Staten Island businesses.

chapters.summary: Carr contends there is still time to pause and minimize unintended consequences for Staten Island, arguing the borough is unique. Anderson responds that the bill being heard on implementation day, after seven years of work and investment by carters, indicates it is too late for that conversation.
chapters.title: Carr presses on timing and pause for Staten Island CWZ

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Well, a fifth is a pretty substantial number of folks.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I admit it's not a majority, but I think that's a large number of folks who are now looking for a new carter when they didn't feel the need to choose one.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that, you know, it's about whether or not I or the department or anyone in government wants to substitute our judgment for who the best carding service for a customer is.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that the.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: The concerns that I have is that it's going to end up harming businesses.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that I understand their price controls, but I think ultimately at the end of the day that we're taking away these choices from consumers and we're boxing out local businesses that have been serving these communities for some time.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that that's why we're at this point, because we're on the precipice of implementation, as you say, but there's still two months, as you say, for folks to choose one of the new three carters.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So I don't think that there's no time to turn things around and kind of take a pause and have a conversation.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: About how we can minimize the negative consequences, unintended, I think, for a borough that I think you would admit is unique among the other four.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that there's reasons for us to treat our borough differently.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that's one of the things we've been pushing for as a united delegation is to say Staten Island's different, each borough is different, and there's a way for us to justify rationally a different treatment for different places.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: avoids the what about me problem that you're describing.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I just think, you know, the fact that this bill is being heard on the day that implementation is starting, I think indicates that it's, you know, we have already invested a tremendous amount of effort and thought.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: The Carters have invested a tremendous amount of their time and money into preparing for these, for this program.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And, you know, I think

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: if that were something that Staten Islanders strongly felt two years ago, five years ago, seven years ago when this bill was passed by the city council, that would have been the appropriate time for that conversation.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks about the process by which a third Staten Island awardee was bought out and whether it sets a precedent that buying a carter automatically yields a city contract. Anderson explains the due diligence process, noting pricing and contractual requirements remain unchanged after acquisitions.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez questions Anderson on carter buyout and contract transfer process

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: One of the, thank you member, minority leader Carr for your questions.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: One of the concerns that I have, separated and aside from the Staten Island issue is, I know that Staten Island had a Carter that was changed to, was bought out by another Carter.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And can you explain the process of how they automatically were,

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Yes, they did buy out that third carter that had been the awardee.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: But for the future, one of the concerns that I have is, does that set a precedent that if you buy out any of the other carters, you automatically have a contract with the city?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Could you just explain a little bit more on how that process came to be and for the future, what that process will look like for other times?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I know we were in a bit of a...

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Situation as we were trying to get the CW

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: UZ online for today, but just understanding what that process can look like for the future and if there are any adjustments or amendments that can be made so that it doesn't look like all you have to do is buy somebody out and then you get a city contract.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, so we've actually seen a large amount of merger and acquisition activity in the commercial waste industry since

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: since Local Law 199 passed in 2019, and then also since the awards were done in 2024.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: For example, the first zone that was implemented, the Queen Central Zone, one of the awardees was purchased by another company,

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Royal was purchased by Waste Connections.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: That award was then transferred to the other awardee.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: There's a due diligence and review process that Frank and his staff conduct to ensure that the purchasing company has the financial wherewithal to provide the service that we require,

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: that they're able to meet all of the standards and contractual requirements.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And most importantly, the pricing does not change.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So if one company merges with another or acquires another, the pricing that was in that initial RFP stands as the pricing that they're required to honor.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And so, you know, it's not simply you're buying the award, but you are, you know, purchasing the company and agreeing to...

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: meet all of the same requirements that that predecessor company had committed to.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And that's common across New York City contracts.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We have situations where, and this has happened, for example, with our recycling vendor.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: It's gone through, in the time that I've worked at sanitation for the last 12 years or so, has gone through three different names, three different corporate ownership structures.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: That kind of thing happens in the corporate world, and that kind of thing happens in the entities that we as the city do business with quite regularly.

chapters.summary: Sanchez presses whether DSNY would be open to amending the process so that if a carter cannot fulfill its promises, the RFP is reopened for bidding. Anderson explains that where open slots exist, DSNY does issue new RFPs, and describes the Staten Island situation where the subcontractor became the prime after acquisition.
chapters.title: Sanchez asks about reopening RFP when a carter cannot fulfill an award

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Is there a world or would it complicate more things?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I think one of the frustrations that the Staten Island businesses are feeling is for that third RFP, folks are feeling like, well, once that company is gone, why didn't we get a chance to then all bid for it again?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Because there are six different carters that already exist on Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Why couldn't I throw my hat in the ring to try and get that now that this person is not doing it?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Would you be open to amending the process of the...

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: If a Carter cannot fulfill its promises that we open up the RFP process committed directly to the agreed upon contract already, just folks get to bid if they want that contract or would that add a crazy

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: level layer to the process?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, I don't know that we would necessarily be able to

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: to implement rules like that.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I think that the kinds of merger and acquisition

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: activity that we've seen in the industry are actually a net positive for the industry because we are seeing that well-capitalized companies that are able to invest in new technology, able to invest in cleaner trucks, able to invest in

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: really advanced worker safety training, are making investments by purchasing other companies.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And I think that's exactly the kind of thing that we want to see in the industry for it to

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: grow and develop and become more sophisticated compared to the industry of the old days, particularly when we started to study the industry.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: There are places where if two companies that are already given awards in a zone merge together and there's no other eligible carters that responded to the RFP,

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: where there may be an open slot, and in that case, we will bid out that open slot.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We are doing that right now in two zones, and I think RFP responses came back a few weeks ago.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We're in the process of evaluating those, and we'll have new awards in the future.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Local Law 199 gives the department pretty broad discretion on how to

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: how to decide when and where to fill zones.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We can either assign the zone contract to an existing carter, we can do a new RFP, we can go back to the list of carters that previously had bid on the zone.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And I think if you look at the original RFP for the Staten Island zone, we had five carters bid on that zone.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: One of the five carters, who's

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: in the back row of this room, already had 15 zones, so they weren't eligible under the language of the law.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: The other four carters, one of them was priority, the other one was century, century bought priority, so they would have been the next in line to get the zone anyway.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So I think any way you wanna sort of

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: slice it, you know, I think we followed the appropriate procedure here and we are confident that Staten Island businesses will get the service they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I'd like to recognize that we've also been joined by Councilmember Ong and I would like to turn it over to Councilmember Maloney for questions.

chapters.summary: Council Member Maloney asks whether DSNY has data on demand for composting facilities in each borough. Anderson explains the size requirement is impractical, noting the Staten Island facility processes 40,000 wet tons and the bill requires nearly five times that in each borough, which is not feasible in Manhattan or most boroughs.
chapters.title: Maloney questions Anderson on Intro 369 composting capacity and demand data

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I am glad to see Intro 75 and your support.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I think enforcement of sanitation from business hours is a sensible solution.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And I wanted to ask about Intro 369, where your testimony indicates that there are

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: concerns around expanding capacity citywide.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And I wanted to know if you have data on the demand in each borough and a study or inquiry into

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: the demand and whether or not these

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: organic waste composting facilities are needed in each borough.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, so I think in general we support the councils

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Intent to expand composting capacity in New York City and in the surrounding metropolitan area.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I think, you know, composting is a very land intensive activity and Councilmember Carr has been out to our Staten Island compost facility with Jen and I

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: and you know that facility processes 40,000 wet tons per year.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We're talking about a facility that's almost five times that size in each borough.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: You know, that's a big facility.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: You couldn't find that space in Manhattan or really realistically in most of the other boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So our opposition to the bill is specifically around the size requirement, the fact that we would have to site one in each borough.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So I think we're open to conversations with the council around where should we go directionally with composting.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I think we are very excited to, now that the administration has transferred some of the

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Rikers properties to DCAS.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We're very excited to work with DCAS to see if we can actually construct a sizable composting facility there.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So I think there's a lot of potential in this space, but the approach of this bill, I think, just was not the right direction.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you for clarifying your position.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And then yield back to the chair.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Councilmember Ung, do you have any questions?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Second round to Councilmember Carr.

chapters.summary: Carr argues the law does not leave DSNY with sufficient options, expressing concern that the current structure incentivizes buyouts to game the system. He asks whether DSNY retains discretion to require a new RFP even after BIC approves an acquisition. Anderson confirms DSNY retains discretion but must have a rational basis.
chapters.title: Carr's second round on RFP process for carter acquisitions

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Commissioner, thank you for explaining the approach to the substitution with respect to the third awardee in the Staten Island zone.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I think one of my concerns is not that the department did anything inconsistent with the law.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: My problem is...

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: The way the law is structured, right?

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that the, what you described, right, I think kind of still leaves me with concerns because I think a new RFP

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: would have been the most appropriate thing because once you've done an RFP and now it actually incentivizes those who may think they're in the queue

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: to be substituted in for a Carter if they're able to do a buyout, the structure of the law is now creating conversations that wouldn't have happened otherwise.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think that when you have an acquisition that comes in the middle of an award period, I think that's a very different thing to assess than

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: what we were looking at where this conversation

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Station was happening in advance of the award coming into effect.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And so I still think that there should be a protocol that requires the department in advance of the implementation of the zone or moving forward in advance of a new award whenever that day comes

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: for there to be a new RFP if there's going to be a potential sale or an awardee isn't going to be activating their award and serving.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I think, and that gives an opportunity for the department to refresh the list of people who are potentially available or companies that are potentially available to execute that award if they get selected.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I appreciate the point you're making, but I think we followed the letter of Local Law 199 in this process and just, I think, fundamentally disagree with that kind of approach.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I think acquisition happens in these kinds of industries.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I think it's not something for us to necessarily shy away from.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So you said the local law gives the agency broad discretion.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Do you believe it gives you the discretion to choose to do a new RFP in these circumstances if you wanted?

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Or if you deemed it appropriate?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Where we've had open awards, we've done new RFPs in other areas.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So in the case of a potential acquisition, you could do another RFP without any amendment to the local law?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Particularly if we denied the contract assignment or if the selected awardee were to pull out of the zone for some reason.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: The situation here was one Carter was...

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Was prepared to start offering the service with the other Carter as a designated Carter, which is also allowed for under the law.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And the acquisition simply inverted those two parties and the Carter that was going to be the subcontractor became the prime.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So it's,

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: From our perspective, it was not a tremendous change.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: But if BIC approves the purchase, do you still retain discretion?

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: No, I know they did, but do you still retain discretion as a department?

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Department not to allow the award to transfer.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Or is it your view that once BIC approves the acquisition?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We retain discretion for sure, but the discretion is subject to some parameters around having a rational basis for whatever direction we go.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Yeah, I think the way the law is structured, it's not leaving you with sufficient options because I think that this is just creating some bad incentives

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: on the industry side for folks who made a bid, they didn't get in, and they see an opportunity to get in because they know that they can game it out, that they're likely, much like in the situation you described,

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: one of the carters wasn't eligible because they'd already gotten enough zones under the law, and they see an opportunity to move up, and I think we have to seek to address that moving forward.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks about the distance organic waste travels before anaerobic digestion and the transfer steps involved. Deputy Commissioner Jen McDonald describes the network of eight contracts and transfer stations, explaining how food and yard waste are separated and sent to compost facilities or digesters.
chapters.title: Sanchez questions Anderson on organic waste travel distance and transfer process

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So I want to talk briefly about Enter 369.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: On average, what is the distance that organic waste must travel from New York City residents before it is anaerobically digested?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And can you describe each of the waste transfers involved in this travel?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Sure, I'll start off and then hand it over to Deputy Commissioner McDonald to talk about all of the specific facilities that we use.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: But in general, waste doesn't actually travel that far to be either composted or

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: process through anaerobic digestion.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We have a number of facilities in New York City that receive that material.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We have facilities in New Jersey that receive that material.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And then they're transferring it on to generally either the Newtown Creek wastewater treatment plant or a wastewater treatment plant that co-digests the material in New Jersey.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So Jen can speak to the specifics of that.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: Yes, thank you, Commissioner and Chair Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: The department issued a procurement in 2023 to secure additional capacity for the receipt, processing, and beneficial use

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: of all the organics collected by DSNY and also those generated by agencies and institutions that we collaborate with, such as the schools and parks.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: So as a result of that procurement, we awarded eight contracts.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: Seven of those contracts have been activated, and they are associated with a variety of transfer stations located throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: So that does minimize the travel distance.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: Once the trucks collect the organic material, it goes to a transfer station.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: One of those, it's actually eight locations.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: Seven of them are transfer stations, and one is the Staten Island Compost Facility that we've spoken about today.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: So we have direct deliveries of collected material from Staten Island and South Brooklyn that go to that facility.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: So overall, citywide, we have a robust network of locations that minimizes the distance that trucks have to travel after collecting organics.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: Once the material is received at the transfer stations, it is sometimes separated by subtype.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: So, for example, we have a very convenient and efficient program whereby we collect food waste and yard waste together at the curb.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: So at some of the transfer stations, they separate the material into piles of yard waste and food waste so that they can process them differentially.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: Sometimes the woody yard waste will go to composting and the organics, the wetter, heavier food waste to co-digestion.

utterances.speaker: Jen McDonald
utterances.text: So it really depends on the transfer station and then from there it will go to compost facilities and or digesters for the beneficial use processing part of the resource recovery pathway.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Awesome.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks whether DSNY has received requests from carters to use marine or rail transfer stations. Anderson says no direct requests but some carters indicated interest during the RFP process, particularly for Upper Manhattan zones. Sanchez also confirms there is only one city-owned rail transfer station, on Staten Island.
chapters.title: Sanchez questions on Intro 367: carter interest in city transfer stations

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And speaking about transfer stations, we're going to turn over to Intro 367.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Has the department received requests from carters to use the department's marine transfer stations or rail transfer stations?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: If so, which carters have made this request and which facilities were they interested in?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: We haven't received requests, but we did when we did the RFP part two for the commercial waste zone awards, we did ask carters to indicate if they would be interested in using our transfer stations.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Some of them did indicate interest, particularly those that will eventually service the Upper East Side zone.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I think there were some of those and some of the other zones in Upper Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So certainly once we have completed implementation, we'll reach out to those.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And how many city-owned or operated rail transfer stations are there in New York City?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Just one.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Just one.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Staten Island, which is not currently permitted for commercial waste, so we'd have to do a permanent modification.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay, cool.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks how enforcement resource needs would change under Intro 75. Anderson says needs would not change but resources would be used more effectively, giving the example of businesses like bars that are not open during the current enforcement windows.
chapters.title: Sanchez questions on Intro 75 enforcement resource needs

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And in terms of entry of 75, can you describe how

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: how the department's enforcement resource needs would change if this bill were to be enacted?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, so I don't think our enforcement resource needs would change, but we'd be able to use those resources more effectively in order to conduct that enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: And I think one of the examples that our team talks about a lot is

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: You can have an area, so the two one-hour periods are different district by district.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: You could have a district where one of the commercial routing hours is 10 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: To 11 a.m., but a business may not open, you know, let's say they're a bar or something like that that doesn't open until noon or later.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: That means that during that 10 to 11 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Window, we can't even write them a ticket because they're not open.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So what this would allow us to do is go to that business anytime they're open, observe the condition, and then issue a violation.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: So we'd be able to...

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Use our existing enforcement resources more effectively.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: No questions from them.

chapters.summary: Sanchez dismisses DSNY, opens the hearing for public testimony, and reminds attendees of decorum rules including no singing on Zoom, two-minute speaking limits, and procedures for submitting written testimony.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez transitions to public testimony and gives instructions

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I want to thank you all for your time, and I think we can open it up for the public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Stay cool and enjoy the July 4th weekend.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Have a summer.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: River?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do I have to check in for zoning?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do I have to check in for zoning?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I don't have to go.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I don't care.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay, I now open the hearing for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I remind all members of the public that this is a government proceeding and that decorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: That includes no singing on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant at Arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with the Sergeant at Arms and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: When recognized, you will have two minutes to speak on the legislation on our agenda today.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: If you have a written statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the Sergeant at Arms.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You may also email written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I will now call the first panel.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Can I please have Josh Raff, Justin Wood, also known as Justin number one, and Joyce Bialik.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: Really?

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Josh Raff of Action Environmental Services supports Intros 75, 356, 367, and 369, strongly supports Intro 353's working group as a productive stakeholder forum, and staunchly opposes Intro 911A, saying it undermines Local Law 199 and would set a harmful precedent by disrupting the Staten Island zone before launch.
chapters.title: Josh Raff (Action Environmental) supports CWZ working group, opposes Intro 911A

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: My name is Josh Hariff, and I serve as the Marcus Vice President for New York City Action Environmental Services, a subsidiary of Interstate Waste.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to deliver testimony regarding the bills considered during today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: With respect to bills 75, 356, 367, and 369, Action Carding supports reasonable legislation that encourages street cleanliness, expands access to composting, and promotes economically feasible disposal options.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Action is poised to support commercial businesses with relevant compliance education in alignment with the progression of these bills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Furthermore, Action Carding staunchly supports Bill 353 introduced by Councilwoman Nurse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: The creation of a commercial waste zones working group would structure a healthy dialogue amongst critical stakeholders who are responsible for the successful realization of local law 199's key objectives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Awarded carters engage with the DSNY to successfully onboard zones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: However, there are limited opportunities to provide or absorb more holistic feedback amongst industry colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: This working group would be a productive forum.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: To discuss customers' experiences transitioning to CWZ service agreements, operational best practices and technologies that prioritize safety, and opportunities for efficiency improvements within an evolving regulatory landscape.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: In contrast, Action Carding staunchly opposes the adoption of Bill 911A, which would unnecessarily alter the operating framework and timely implementation of the CWZ program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: This bill undermines Local Law 199's intended goals by diluting the required caliber for CWZ awardees and neglecting the DSNY's ability to course-correct underperforming zones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Staten Island's residents and businesses deserve to benefit from reduced truck traffic, vigilant safety standards, and transparency in the same regard as other boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Therefore, Action Carding encourages this committee to avoid establishing a harmful precedent by disrupting the program's potential before the zone even launches.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Josh Raff
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Josh.

chapters.summary: Joyce Bialik of the Manhattan Solid Waste Advisory Board and WE Act for Environmental Justice proposes linking city composting support to healthy food access, suggesting the city map existing farms and gardens, subsidize new growing and composting facilities in underserved areas, and ensure contamination-free compost for nutritious produce.
chapters.title: Joyce Bialik on composting for healthy food access in low-income neighborhoods

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair and dog.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: My name is Joyce Bialik, and I'm a member of the Manhattan Solid Waste Advisory Board, as well as We Act for Environmental Justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: I'm here today to suggest how the city's support of composting can also support

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: one of the values of the mayor's grocery stores, namely making healthy foods accessible to low-income residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: Many of the farms and gardens currently in our city do their own composting and use the compost to grow fruits and vegetables.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: If clean, wholesome food scraps are used to make clean compost, we get clean, wholesome, nutritious fruits and vegetables.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: Studies have shown that some of the chemicals found in produce not grown organically are forever chemicals and that small children and pregnant women are particularly vulnerable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: Currently, the city's farms and gardens are not distributed evenly across the city, nor are they selling their produce directly to the public or using some of their land to increase compost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: To create compost, the city could build a system that maps the current distribution of these farms and community gardens and subsidizes the development of additional

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: of additional

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: growing and composting facilities wherever possible in areas where they are lacking.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: The city also should guarantee ongoing support for the compost facilities that currently exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: Priority for the farms, gardens, and compost facilities would be given to low-income neighborhoods, and the city would develop a means for each customer to pay directly to the farm or garden

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: at a rate commensurate to that used in the city's grocery stores.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: The city would monitor the growing and selling to ensure the availability of healthy, affordable products that are grown only with contamination-free compost from clean, wholesome food scraps.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Joyce Bialik
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thanks so much, Joyce.

chapters.summary: Justin Wood of New York Lawyers for the Public Interest strongly supports Intro 367 as an environmental justice priority and Intro 369 for local composting capacity. He opposes Intro 911A, warning it would disrupt CWZ benefits and potentially invite litigation from awarded haulers.
chapters.title: Justin Wood (NYLPI) supports Intros 367 and 369, opposes 911A

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: Thank you so much for holding this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: And congratulations to you and Majority Leader Abreu and the Council on the passage of Intro 31 last night.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: We strongly support that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: And of course, on the passage of a robust city budget.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: My name is Justin Wood, Director of Policy at New York Lawyers for the Public Interest, and we're a member of Transform and Don't Trash New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: As another summer of extreme heat descends on us as we speak, the federal government continues an assault on renewable energy, climate science, public health, and the very concept of environmental justice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: So it's in that context that we offer this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: Strong support for intros 367 and 369 introduced by Councilmember Nurse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: Intro 367, to accept commercial waste at marine and rail facilities owned by the city, is a major and decades-old priority for environmental justice communities that continue to bear the pollution and safety burdens of unjustly cited concentrations of waste transfer stations

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: based on trucks in outer borough communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: These facilities were designed at their inception to accept commercial waste overnight.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: The 2006 Solid Waste Management Plan's environmental impact study explicitly proposed that they be used for this and found there would be minimal impacts on surrounding communities.
