utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: So we'll submit a more detailed written statement, but we strongly support that and any underlying studies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: that sanitation needs to conduct to arrive at any subsidy or price that would be necessary to induce commercial haulers to use these facilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: We similarly strongly support Intro 369, requiring the city to operate at least one major aerobic composting facility in each borough, building on the successful expansion of the Fresh Kills facility we heard about today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: With the passage of Intro 31 and the long-awaited implementation and enforcement of commercial and residential

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: recycling rules, now is the time to begin planning to process far more organic waste locally and produce rich soil fertilizer for our parks, farms, and gardens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: Finally, if I may, we strongly oppose Intro 911A, which would retroactively delay and undermine implementation of CWZ in Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: Simply put, Staten Islanders, and I happen to be a resident of the borough, residents, sanitation workers, and businesses deserve the immediate benefits that started today of the CWZ system that was studied, debated, and legislated almost a decade ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: Safer streets, cleaner air, recycling, composting, food donation services, and an affordable price structure that ensures Staten Island businesses can save money by diverting more waste from landfills are all good things.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: I would just add that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: If this bill were to pass, we would be concerned that the city would be subject to almost certainly to litigation by the haulers that have already been awarded these zones and implementation began today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: And we would urge the council not to disrupt the good things that are happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: Our partners in organized labor at the Teamsters, for example, have been able to organize substantial numbers of workers in this historically dangerous and exploitative industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Wood
utterances.text: So thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thanks so much, Justin.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And with that, this first panel is relieved.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Now I'd like to bring up Eric Goldstein, Rhonda Kayser, and Jamiri Rush.

utterances.speaker: Gregory Anderson
utterances.text: I hope I said that right.

chapters.summary: Eric Goldstein of the Natural Resources Defense Council calls compost processing capacity the most important issue before the committee, strongly supporting Intros 369 and 367. He opposes Intro 911A as counter to the council's intent and supports Intro 356 for community composter fees.
chapters.title: Eric Goldstein (NRDC) on composting capacity, transfer stations, and 911A

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Thank you for having us at this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Eric Goldstein from the Natural Resources Defense Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: I'll summarize our written testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: We strongly support Intro 369.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: The issue of compost processing capacity could be the most important issue to come before this committee this year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: This could be a legacy issue for you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Here's why.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Composting organics are the single largest portion of the waste stream.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: A third of our waste is food scraps, yard waste, food soil, paper.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: If it goes to landfills, it creates methane.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: If it goes to incinerators, its high moisture content fouls up the burning process and generates localized pollution.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: If it goes to...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Sewage treatment plants for co-digestion, while that may be better than landfilling and incineration,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: mixing it with sewage sludge means that you cannot compost the digestate, and so it's not the highest and best use for compostable material.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: We definitely need more capacity for composting in city, which also brings jobs and could save money in our exporting costs over time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: We hope to be able to work with you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: and Sandy Nurse to get this bill in shape where it can be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: But we do say that it's hard to find the more important issue for the long-term sustainability of our system than to address the issue of getting additional composting capacity in the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Very quickly, we also strongly support Intro 367.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: This is an environmental justice issue that has lingered for years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Three communities get the overwhelming bulk of commercial waste.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Opening up the modern marine transfer stations in the city's own rail facilities to commercial waste could alleviate that burden.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: We know that Commissioner Anderson has the expertise, the staff, and the authority to get this work done if he is authorized to do so by the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Finally, we strongly oppose Intro 9-11.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: This bill would directly run counter to the intent of the council when it passed the waste zone legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: It was debated for years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: In fact, only one of the three Staten Island council members voted against this introduction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: So in other words, there was support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: They had the opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: There was support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: The program is ongoing now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: And it would also set a horrible precedent to exempt one borough from

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: from citywide legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: So we urge that that program be sent to the landfills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: And last but not least, we also strongly support 356, allowing community composting operations to charge for organic waste drop-offs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: We explain why in our testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Goldstein
utterances.text: Thank you for your attention.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Appreciate it.

chapters.summary: Jameer Rush of Cafeteria Culture describes a pilot program where one compost coordinator diverted over a ton of food scraps from four schools, arguing Intro 369 would enable scalable local composting that reduces truck traffic and supports education and community engagement.
chapters.title: Jameer Rush (Cafeteria Culture) on school composting pilot and Intro 369

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Good morning and thank you for the opportunity for me to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: My name is Jameer Rush and I work with Cafeteria Culture and I urge you to pass and show 369, which will require composting processing capacity in every borough of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: This year, Caviteer Culture launched a pilot program that showed what local composting can accomplish.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: I was hired as a compost cranter to collect cafeteria food scraps, educate students about composting, and transport those scraps to nearby community composting sites or on-site tumblers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Since late October 2005, I have led students to divert more than one ton of food scraps from just four schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: PS 46, PS 34, PS 15, and PS 30.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Every pound of compost we use in the school gardens we created at these schools comes from compost powers, locals, composting sites.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: So instead of trucking our food waste across the city and burning our other communities, we turn these food scraps into a local resource that nourishes our gardens, supports environmental education, and strengthens our neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: This is exactly why Intro 369 matters.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Local composting reduces unnecessary truck traffic, builds resilient neighborhood infrastructure, and creates opportunities for education, jobs, and community engagement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Our pilot proves that integrating school cafeterias into neighborhood composting is practical, efficient, and scalable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Just one compost coordinator, me, working with four diverted, whoa,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Just one compost coordinator working with four schools diverted more than a ton of organic matter in a matter of months.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Imagine how every school in every borough could achieve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Whoa.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Imagine what every school in every borough could achieve with the right infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: We ask you to please pass Intro 369 and invest in a composting system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: That keeps resources where they are generated and benefits every New York City community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jameer Rush
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Whoa.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Buzz it, you know.

chapters.summary: Rhonda Keyser explains that schools generate large consistent volumes of food scraps, making them the most efficient places to recover organics. She describes students' hands-on learning and sense of ownership at compost sites, urging the council to pass Intro 369.
chapters.title: Rhonda Keyser (Cafeteria Culture) on efficiency of school food scrap collection

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: Hi, I'm Rhonda Kaiser.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: I'm Program and Policy Director for Cafeteria Culture.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: Our team works together with Mr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: Rush to evaluate and to shape the food scraps collection pilot.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: And one of the biggest lessons from our pilot is how efficient it is to collect food scraps.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: from schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: We realize that our opinion differs from DSNY about whether this is possible, intro 369 is possible, but it's a new idea, and new ideas require conversational generosity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: So that's what we're hoping to engage in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: And that's what we're hoping to receive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: And because in just four schools, one compost coordinator diverted more than a ton of food scraps in only a few months, reaching that same amount of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: people, individuals, by asking individual households to separate and set out their food scraps would require educating and engaging hundreds, if not thousands, of families.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: Schools generate large, consistent volumes of food scraps every day, making it the easiest and most efficient places to recover organics.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: And then by integrating schools into neighborhood composting systems, students learn how and they learn why and actually how to divert their food scraps through hands-on education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: And while teaching them about recycling programs and the virtue of separating their waste is a great idea, actually giving them the value of the benefit of their...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: so-called waste, but actually resources, they actually feel the difference.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: And it's palpable what students can understand about it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: And I've said this many times, but our students from PSMS 46 visited Polo Grounds and they looked around and they said to Domingo, do you own this site?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: And he said, this is funded by taxes, so technically we all own this site.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: and the kids beamed to think that they owned a site, that they could actually make a difference and make a huge difference.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: These are students, some of our most vulnerable students, who could make a huge difference where the Brown Bin program doesn't exist.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: Our program disrupts the truck-based and harmful and polluting industrial model, and it offers a new solution and another world is possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rhonda Keyser
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And with that, this panel is relieved.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Can I please bring up Mary Ellen Sullivan, Matthew Chivello, and Wendy Frank?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Mary Ellen Sullivan of the Manhattan, Brooklyn, Bronx, and Queens Solid Waste Advisory Boards strongly supports Intro 369, warning that co-digesting organics with sewage creates contaminated biosolids. She recommends re-establishing a compost siting task force, prioritizing nonprofit composters, and integrating environmental justice and workforce development.
chapters.title: Mary Ellen Sullivan (SWABs) on Intro 369 and biosolids concerns

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: I am Mary Ellen Sullivan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: The Manhattan, Brooklyn, Bronx, and Queens Solid Waste Advisory Board strongly support Intro 369 and its goal to establish local composting capacity in each of the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: Intro 369 provides a clear and practical framework for developing a resilient, equitable, and locally-based organics processing infrastructure consistent with the SWAB's ongoing recommendation that New York City should prioritize local composting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: New York City residents and commercial businesses generate almost 2 million tons of organic material annually, with the vast majority of this being sent to landfills, incinerators, or co-digestion facilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: New York City is losing out on an opportunity to turn our organic waste into something valuable, new jobs, additional tax revenue, reduced environmental harm, and locally made compost to apply in our parks and neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: Co-digesting organics with sewage creates more sewage sludge, a waste that is difficult and expensive to dispose of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: Current DEP policy is to maximize beneficial use of biosolid.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: That means land application.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: Yet municipalities across New York State and the entire country are waking up to the shock of land polluted by biosolids, and they are beginning to oppose this practice and take action.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: The policy of our own New York Farm Bureau is to stand in opposition to land application of biosolids that have detectable levels of PFAS, PFOS, and heavy metals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: The solution is obvious, compost our waste organically.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: To strengthen Intro 369, we respectfully recommend the following modifications.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: Re-establish the compost siting task force so that Section 2B of Intro 369 can be realized.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: Explicitly include and prioritize nonprofit organizations, including but not limited to the New York City Compost Network.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: as eligible entities to participate in this activity, ensure that facility siting decisions incorporate environmental justice considerations, and avoid the environmental justice errors of our current waste system,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: integrate robust public education to the rollout, and integrate workforce development programs into this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mary Ellen Sullivan
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Matthew Civello, chair of the Manhattan SWAB, supports Intro 367 as building on the waste equity law to reduce truck traffic in overburdened communities. He requests DSNY make public its findings on carter interest in using marine transfer stations and a feasibility evaluation.
chapters.title: Matthew Civello (Manhattan SWAB) on Intro 367 and waste equity

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: I'm Matthew Savello, chair of the Manhattan Solid Waste Advisory Board, and I thank you for this opportunity to provide testimony at today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: The Solid Waste Advisory Board support Intro 367, which would require the Department of Sanitation to accept commercial solid waste

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: at city-owned marine and rail transfer stations and publicly report the amount and type of waste received.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: Implemented carefully, Intro 367 could significantly reduce waste transfer burdens and truck traffic in overburdened communities of Brooklyn Community District 1, Queens Community District 12, Bronx Community Districts 1 and 2.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: Intro 367 builds directly on local law 152, the waste equity law, enacted to reduce the disproportionate impacts of truck traffic and waste processing in environmental justice communities,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: and with a hope of preventing new neighborhoods from becoming the next dumping grounds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: Intro 367 could now help provide meaningful relief

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: to those communities that have carried those burdens for decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: Before this committee in 2023, the Department of Sanitation stated that it had asked commercial waste zone proposers whether they were interested in using the city's marine transfer stations and that the RFP process would help determine that demand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: We respectfully request that those findings be made public along with a public evaluation of the feasibility and benefits of accepting commercial waste at these facilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: A better utilization of the city's marine and rail transfer stations as suggested by Intro 367

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: can improve the distribution of commercial waste across the city of New York, reduce truck traffic in historically overburdened neighborhoods, and help fulfill the promise of the waste equity law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: Again, thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

chapters.summary: Wendy Frank supports Intro 356 for community composter financial sustainability and Intro 353's working group, recommending expanded membership including commercial waste customers and micro-haulers, annual public reports, and greater transparency. She also reminds the committee about composting at large citywide events.
chapters.title: Wendy Frank (SWABs) on Intros 356 and 353 working group recommendations

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: Hi there, Wendy Frank from the Manhattan Solid Waste Advisory Board.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: The Manhattan, Brooklyn, Bronx, and Queens Solid Waste Advisory Boards support both Intro 356 and Intro 353 and commend the council for advancing these important measures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: Intro 356 would provide community composters with a more sustainable and diversified funding model by allowing them to charge for food scrap collections and sell the compost they produce.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: The swabs recommend expanding the bill to also allow community composters to charge local entities for food scrap pickup and drop-off services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: This additional flexibility would strengthen the long-term financial sustainability of community compost programs across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: The swabs also support Intro 353, establishing a commercial waste zone working group with representation from waste industry labor, environmental organizations, environmental justice advocates, and

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: micro haulers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: This diverse membership will ensure that commercial waste zones achieve the objectives established under local law 199 of 2019.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: We recommend the working group issue an annual public report evaluating the implementation of commercial waste zones, including their impacts on truck traffic, emissions, public, and worker safety, and other key performance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: At the conclusion of its work, the group will also issue recommendations for improving the program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: Finally, we recommend adding commercial waste customers to the working group and providing opportunities for solid waste advisory boards and other stakeholders to observe meetings.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: Greater transparency and broader participation will strengthen both the working group and the successful implementation of commercial waste zones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: Thank you for your consideration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: And also, you may remember me from the last hearing when...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: I informed you about bananas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Wendy Frank
utterances.text: And when you have a large citywide event with over 40,000 people and you don't compost those bananas, it's bananas.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And this panel is relieved.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And for our next and last panel, can I please have Domingo Morales, Justin Green, and Aaron Wright.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I want to end with Aaron.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Domingo Morales of Compost Power, operating eight compost sites on public housing campuses, supports Intro 369 for local composting capacity and Intro 356 for financial sustainability. He describes a hyperlocal model of collecting, processing, and distributing compost on NYCHA campuses for free food and education.
chapters.title: Domingo Morales (Compost Power) on NYCHA composting and Intros 369, 356

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Domingo, you can begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: Thank you for having me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: I'm Domingo Morales from Compost Power.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: We operate eight compost sites on public housing campuses throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: And I'm here to support Intro 369.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: I think it would behoove New York City to have more local compost in New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: I think the seven transfer stations that we do have, it's like a double route where we have to take it to a transfer station, emulsify it, turn it to a liquid form, and then take it to a water treatment plant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: And that just doubles the amount of trucking that we need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: Also, there's a lot of biosolids that end up in landfill anyway.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: I think with the compost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: Sites that we have in New York City, we're able to keep a hyperlocal model, like at our public housing campuses, we're able to collect the waste on campus, process it on campus, distribute the compost on campus, grow free food for

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: residents, provide environmental education for young adults, as well as the kids who attend the local schools in the community, and even in some cases, seniors are coming out to sift through finished compost and use their finished compost on their gardening efforts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: I think public housing has the second largest tree canopy in New York City, so it's beneficial to have local processing sites on public housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: And I think with Intro 369, Department of Sanitation can't do it by themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: So they'll need to tap into other community composting support networks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: They'll need to partner with New York City Housing Authority.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: They'll need to partner with Parks Department in order to get the space to build these composting sites that we need.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: So it will provide a more holistic approach to organic waste in New York City, and it will provide more of a community tapped in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: So I'm in support for that bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: I also think Intro 356, we're in support for that as well because those are rules that were in place when Department of Sanitation controlled the New York City Compost Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: They no longer fund the New York City Compost Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: We now have the New York City Community Compost Network that's funded by City Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Domingo Morales
utterances.text: And I think if we allow people to explore different options for funding, we'll be able to make community compost a little bit more sustainable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Matthew Civello
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your testimony.

chapters.summary: Justin Green of Big Reuse supports expanding composting capacity, citing potential sites at NYCHA, parks, community gardens, and brownfield sites. He argues against relying on anaerobic digestion due to contaminated biosolids and advocates for compost as green infrastructure that improves water absorption and plant growth.
chapters.title: Justin Green (Big Reuse) on expanding composting infrastructure citywide

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: On now on hi I'm Justin green I'm the executive director of big Reeves thanks for having us all here to testify when a second pretty much everything Domingo said

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: both of us have been composting in New York City for a while now and we can see the capacity that's available in the city to grow green infrastructure to increase composting

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: capacity throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: So at NYCHA Lake Domingos piloted and run for years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: In parks, we're working with Central Parks to set up a site, or we have a site operating in Central Park now to compost all their landscaping waste.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: We can expand that to other parks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: We all work, both of us work in community gardens and helping them maximize their composting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: And there's large-scale brownfield sites in northern Astoria, in East Williamsburg, that are huge brownfield sites that could be

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: not great for building residential because of the brownfield nature, but could be used for green infrastructure projects like this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: And there are examples of these kind of large-scale capacity systems being installed in buildings, enclosed, so there's no odor issues going to the outside community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: So it's possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: If we can dream big, we can make this happen in the city, reduce our impact on other communities from

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: exporting our waste, reduce our reliance on anaerobic digestion, which as a lot of speakers have talked about, reduces

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: unusable biosolids at this point, and not the compost we had all fought to have be produced through all of our work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: The biosolids are contaminated with everything that goes down into the sewer, PFAS, microplastics, anything else everyone pours down the drain in the city ends up in our biosolids at the sewage treatment plant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: So it's not particularly usable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: But compost is, and with growing resiliency needs in the city,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: And compost provides sort of greater water absorption, greater plant growth, and other green infrastructure improvements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: And I think we could do it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Justin Green
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: No better way to end our hearing today than with...

chapters.summary: Erin Wright representing Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso urges passage of Intro 353's working group and Intro 367 for marine transfer stations, expresses concern about Intro 911A's precedent, and provides written comments on Intro 369. She notes positive early CWZ implementation results on safety, unionization, and costs.
chapters.title: Erin Wright (BP Reynoso's office) on CWZ bills and Intro 369

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair, and thank you for holding this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: My name is Erin Wright, and I'm here representing Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: He appreciates your ongoing attention to the rollout of commercial waste zones, a program he created during his time in your position.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: When fully implemented, this program will address issues with the private carting industry by limiting vehicle miles traveled, increasing enforcement of dangerous driving,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: incentivizing the replacement of out-of-date fleets, requiring more attention to worker safety, and increasing diversion of waste from landfills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Despite unfortunate delays and our continued calls for DSNY to speed up the program's rollout, the BP has been pleased with its implementation so far.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Initial information indicates that increased enforcement in the active zones is leading to improved safety outcomes, that more workers now have union protection, and that the program has not caused costs to rise dramatically as some have predicted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: I'm here to provide comments on the three bills being heard today in relation to CWZ as well as Intro 369.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: First, Intro 353 would create a CWZ working group.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: The Council has been considering this bill for many years and it's time to finally pass it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Having experts at the table from both within and outside the industry can only help guide the process and ensure its continued success.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Intro 367 would require DSNY to establish a plan

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: for accepting commercial waste at the marine transfer stations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: BP Reynoso originally introduced this bill when he was at City Council, and unfortunately DSNY continues to slow roll this necessary measure, which would remove dangerous and polluting waste trucks from our streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Thanks to advocacy by the EJ community, marine transfer stations,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Remove waste by barge and rail rather than long haul truck.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: This bill would push DSNY to create a clear plan that would support our EJ communities and we urge the council to pass it quickly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Intro 911A would increase the number of carters allowed to participate in CWZ in Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: BP Reynoso has concerns about this proposal and the precedent it could set for other zones citywide.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Further comments are provided in writing on 369 in addition to a copy of our report on the bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erin Wright
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I just want to show everyone what experience looks like.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: We really thank you for all your testimony and your reports and Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso for all of his work on this committee and the work that he will continue to do.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And with that, this panel is dismissed.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And we thought we were done.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: We are not done.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: They have joined us on Zoom from the ether.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Lou, can you hear us?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Lou Dubuque of the National Waste and Recycling Association strongly supports Intro 353's working group, supports expanding marine and rail use with concerns about mandatory participation under Intro 367, and notes written testimony was submitted on Intro 911A. His oral testimony is cut off by the time limit.
chapters.title: Lewis Dubuque (NWRA) on Intro 353, 367, and 911A

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Yes, I can hear everyone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Sanchez and members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: My name is Lou Dubuque, Vice President for the Northeast Region for the National Waste and Recycling Association.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: NWRA represents the private companies that collect and manage commercial waste and recyclables throughout New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Our members will serve customers in 19 of the city's 20 commercial waste zones when fully implemented.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: I want to thank you for the opportunity to testify in intros 353, 367, and 911A.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: First, NWRA strongly supports Intro 353, establishing a commercial waste zone working group.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Two years ago, I testified before this committee in support of creating a formal stakeholder working group because we believed a program as large and complex as the CWZ would require ongoing collaboration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: As implementation has progressed, that has proved to be exactly the case.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: The CWZ program is unlike anything else in the country.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: While there have been challenges, our industry remains fully committed to making it successful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Our members have invested millions of dollars in new equipment, facilities, employees, and infrastructure to help achieve the city's goals of improving safety, reducing traffic, lowering emissions, and providing reliable services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: A permanent working group will allow DSNY, industry, labor, elected officials, and other stakeholders to identify challenges early and develop practical solutions together and continue improving the program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: over time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: We respectfully urge the committee to approve Intro 353.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Regarding Intro 367, NRA supports expanding environmentally responsible transportation options, including greater use of marine and rail.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Those are all goals we share.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: However, we have serious concerns that participation becomes mandatory.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: During the CWZ procurement, companies developed bids, negotiated disposal agreements, invested in infrastructure, and established customer pricing based on the rules that existed at the time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Requiring companies after contract...

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, your time expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Okay, we've submitted written testimony earlier, so you'll have the chance to read that there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Lewis Dubuque
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks if there are any additional public comments, hearing none, wishes everyone a happy summer and adjourns until September.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez closes the hearing

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Are there any other takers for comments at this time?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Going once, going twice.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Hearing none.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Happy summer, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I will see you in September.

summary: Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management holds a hearing on six bills covering sidewalk cleanliness enforcement, commercial waste zone working group, community composting fees, commercial waste at city transfer stations, per-borough composting facilities, and Staten Island CWZ expansion and delay.

DSNY Commissioner Gregory Anderson testifies in support of Intro 75, welcomes the working group concept in Intro 353, deems Intro 356 unnecessary, and opposes Intros 367, 369, and 911A citing implementation, feasibility, and program integrity concerns.

Public witnesses from environmental organizations, solid waste advisory boards, composting groups, and the waste industry largely support expanding composting capacity and the CWZ working group, while most oppose Intro 911A's disruption of the Staten Island commercial waste zone.
title: Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management
